title: Artifacts and Technical Considerations at Contrast-enhanced US
abstract: Contrast-enhanced US (CEUS), similar to other radiologic modalities, requires specific technical considerations and is subject to image artifacts. These artifacts may affect examination quality, negatively impact diagnostic accuracy, and decrease user comfort when using this emerging technique. Some artifacts are related to commonly known gray-scale US artifacts that can also appear on the contrast-only image (tissue-subtracted image obtained with the linear responses from background tissues nulled). These may include acoustic shadowing and enhancement; reverberation, refraction, and reflection; and poor penetration. Other artifacts are exclusive to CEUS owing to the techniques used for contrast mode image generation and the unique properties of the microbubbles that constitute ultrasound-specific contrast agents (UCAs). UCA-related artifacts may appear on the contrast-only image, the gray-scale image, or various Doppler mode images. Artifacts related to CEUS may include nonlinear artifacts and unintentional microbubble destruction resulting in pseudowashout. The microbubbles themselves may result in specific artifacts such as pseudoenhancement, signal saturation, and attenuation and shadowing and can confound the use of color and spectral Doppler US. Identifying and understanding these artifacts and knowing how to mitigate them may improve the quality of the imaging study, increase user confidence, and improve patient care. The authors review the principles of UCAs and the sound-microbubble interaction, as well as the technical aspects of image generation. Technical considerations, including patient positioning, depth, acoustic window, and contrast agent dose, also are discussed. Specific artifacts are described, with tips on how to identify and, if necessary, apply corrective measures, with the goal of improving examination quality. © RSNA, 2022 Online supplemental material and the slide presentation from the RSNA Annual Meeting are available for this article.
rawBodyBlocks: Introduction The introduction of ultrasound-specific contrast agents (UCAs) has led to a renaissance in US, allowing new imaging and interventional techniques that previously were not possible with US. Contrast-enhanced US (CEUS) leverages the inherent benefits of a portable, widely available, and cost-effective modality with UCAs that offer exceptional safety and other unique benefits compared with contrast agents used in CT and MRI (1). CEUS has been used worldwide for years, with several U.S. Food and Drug Administration–approved UCAs in the United States and reimbursement billing codes now available. With the increasing use of CEUS, it is important that radiologists recognize artifacts unique to this modality to help improve image quality, avoid misinterpretations, and develop comfort and confidence in performing the technique (2). Microbubble Formulation and Pharmacokinetics UCAs consist of small particles that are approximately the size of red blood cells (3–5 µm) (3). These particles, commonly known as microbubbles, are composed of an inert, high-molecular-weight, poorly dissolvable fluorocarbon gas core encapsulated in a biocompatible lipid or protein shell. The gas is excreted by means of exhalation, while the phospholipids or proteins of the shell are processed by the reticuloendothelial system. Microbubble clearance is rapid, with a half-life of several minutes; this allows multiple injections during a single examination, providing the opportunity to image multiple organs and characterize multiple lesions (4–8). Subsequent contrast agent doses can generally be administered after two to three half-lives (or about 10 minutes). Purposeful microbubble destruction can accelerate contrast agent clearance. Although U.S. Food and Drug Administration dose limits exist, given their short half-life, higher total administered doses can be used off-label. TEACHING POINT Microbubbles are too large to pass through the vessel endothelium and are purely intravascular contrast agents. Unlike iodinated and gadolinium-based contrast agents that diffuse out of the vascular space, leading to delayed extravascular interstitial accumulation and enhancement, microbubbles function as a pure blood pool agent (Fig 1) (9). This quality allows real-time angiographic imaging of vessels and perfused tissues at high temporal and spatial resolution. UCAs may also be injected into other cavities and spaces and typically will not intravasate into the blood pool. UCAs are not excreted by the kidneys, so urographic imaging may be achieved only by means of direct injection into the urinary tract. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 1. Diagram illustrating intravenously injected radiologic contrast agents shows iodinated and gadolinium molecules exiting the vascular space and entering the extravascular interstitial space, while microbubble particles, like red blood cells, are too large and remain within the vascular space, functioning as a pure blood pool agent. Download as PowerPoint Microbubble-Ultrasound Interaction An ultrasound pulse is a mechanical wave composed of compression and rarefaction sequences at frequencies in the range of 1–20 MHz. Microbubbles contract and expand (ie, oscillate) when exposed to ultrasound at specific frequencies (resonance), usually 1–3 MHz. The degree of oscillation is dependent on the peak pressure. At pressures lower than 100 kPa, microbubbles demonstrate symmetric contraction and rarefaction, also known as linear oscillation (Fig 2). Between peak pressures of 100 and 500 kPa, there is a nonlinear response, meaning the degree of rarefaction is out of proportion to the degree of compression, resulting in a several-fold change in the diameter of a microbubble (10–12). This nonlinear oscillation generates strong harmonic and ultraharmonic frequencies in higher and lower multiples of the transmitted (fundamental) frequency. Most tissues demonstrate a predominantly linear response and produce return echoes at the same frequency as the transmitted pulse. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 2. Diagram illustrates microbubble response to ultrasound pulses of various pressures (power). (A) At low pressure (very low mechanical index [MI]), microbubbles contract and rarefact proportional to the pressure wave (linear oscillation). (B) At slightly higher pressures (but still low MI), microbubble oscillation is nonlinear, meaning rarefaction is out of proportion to compression. This produces strong harmonics of the fundamental frequency. (C) At even higher pulse pressures (high MI), microbubbles become unstable and fall apart, releasing the gas core. This phenomenon may be unintentional or purposeful, resulting in loss of the microbubble-related signal. Download as PowerPoint Another unique physical property of microbubbles is their fragility. At peak pressures greater than 500 kPa, the degree of microbubble oscillation disrupts the shell, with immediate release of the gas core and loss of signal. Therefore, a US machine’s contrast mode relies on low-power imaging, as measured by using the mechanical index (MI), a manufacturer-specific estimate of absorbed acoustic pressure. At a low MI (<0.1), microbubbles demonstrate optimal oscillation to generate a signal while minimizing microbubble destruction. However, a higher MI (>0.5) leads to accelerated microbubble destruction. High-MI techniques may be used to purposefully clear microbubbles from the field of view; this is known as a burst, or flash, whereby a short rapid succession of high-power pulses is transmitted (Fig 3; Movies 1, 2). This technique facilitates a brief high-signal image acquisition, during which microbubbles are destroyed, with subsequent loss of the microbubble-related signal. In addition, this technique can be leveraged to evaluate enhancement patterns and the refill rate of lesions; this is especially useful for characterizing rapidly enhancing liver lesions. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 3. Purposeful microbubble destruction in a 61-year-old man with a history of urothelial cancer and transaminitis, in whom multiple hepatic lesions (found to be cysts) were evaluated on dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the right liver lobe. (A) Images obtained 2 minutes 39 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride lipid-type A microspheres (Lumason, Bracco Diagnostics) show fairly homogeneous enhancement of the hepatic parenchyma. (B) The US system’s burst, or flash, function is activated, resulting in a marked increase in signal throughout the contrast-only image. (C) Immediately afterward, the majority of the signal throughout the liver has been lost (destruction of microbubbles), with early filling of some hepatic vessels (arrows). (D) Turning in an orthogonal plane reveals a dark vertical band (arrowheads) in the original imaging plane, indicating the location of destroyed microbubbles. Download as PowerPoint Movie 1: Flash-reperfusion technique. For lesions or structures with rapid enhancement, such as flash-filling hemangioma and focal nodular hyperplasia, a high-power sequence of ultrasound pulses (ie, burst or flash) can be used to purposefully destroy the microbubbles—and thus the contrast agent–related signal—within the field of view, allowing new microbubbles to enter the structure so that the pattern of enhancement can be re-assessed. In this case, an isoechoic liver lesion has a spoke-wheel pattern of arterial phase hyperenhancement with centrifugal fill-in, characteristic of focal nodular hyperplasia. Download Original Video (48.11 MB) Movie 2: In-plane microbubble destruction can occur after prolonged imaging in a single plane, or after using a high-power sequence of ultrasound pulses (burst or flash), which destroys microbubbles—and thus the contrast agent–related signal—within the field of view. After moving the transducer into the orthogonal plane, a narrow band of relatively low signal signifies the area where microbubbles were destroyed, a form of pseudowashout. Download Original Video (41.77 MB) CEUS Image Generation CEUS image generation relies on the differences in response between microbubbles (nonlinear) and the surrounding tissues (predominantly linear). US devices with contrast agent–specific imaging software take advantage of these differences. Most US unit manufacturers use a combination of phase and amplitude modulation and harmonic filtering to null the linear responses from background tissues and produce contrast-only images (Fig 4). This technique, also known as real-time tissue subtraction, provides exquisite microbubble-specific signal relative to background, allowing the administration of very small contrast agent doses (less than a few milliliters for most agents) and the detection of even a single microbubble in vivo (Movie 3) (13). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 4. Diagram illustrates the phase-inversion technique, one method used in most CEUS modes. In normal scanning, a single transmitted pulse produces backscatter signal from tissues and microbubbles. Most structures, such as the majority of background tissues, respond linearly, meaning that the backscatter signal is centered at fundamental frequency (f), while structures that respond nonlinearly, such as microbubbles, produce strong harmonics (2f, 3f, etc). With phase inversion, two pulses of opposite phase are transmitted nearly simultaneously. The backscatter signal from linearly responding structures is nulled, while the backscatter signal from non–linearly responding structures is relatively amplified. This is one mechanism for achieving background tissue subtraction and creating contrast-only images. Download as PowerPoint Movie 3: Detection of single circulating microbubbles is accomplished by leveraging the nonlinear acoustic response of microbubbles compared with the primarily linear response of the surrounding tissues. By suppressing signal from the background tissues, exquisite signal-to-background is achieved, allowing even a 5-μm microbubble to be detected. Download Original Video (10.15 MB) The contrast-only image is often presented in an orange or sepia hue and can be displayed on its own, in dual-screen mode side by side with a gray-scale image (Figs 5, E1), or as an overlay on the gray-scale image. In contrast mode, the reference gray-scale image is reconstructed from the contrast pulse sequence and is generally of lower quality compared with a standard B-mode gray-scale image. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 5. Dual display mode CEUS in a 49-year-old man with hepatitis C virus and HIV infection, who was found to have sepsis and a liver lesion suspicious for abscess. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the right liver lobe were obtained 57 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres. On the contrast-only image, the lesion (arrows) is nearly completely nonenhancing, indicating nonvascularized material (*), as seen in cysts and other fluid collections such as hematomas and abscesses. Fluid aspiration revealed frankly purulent material. Download as PowerPoint

Technical Parameters and Image Optimization Contrast-enhanced Imaging Mode Settings Achieving an optimal balance between technical imaging parameters, scanning technique, and UCA dose is key to obtaining high-quality CEUS images. To perform high-quality imaging and minimize artifacts, it is important to understand and optimize the technical parameters that influence the contrast mode images (Table 1). Although the specific names and attributes of the adjustable parameters may vary among manufacturers, contemporary contrast settings are generally preoptimized and require minimal, if any, adjustment. User-adjustable settings may include output power, image gain, frame rate, focal zone, transducer frequency, depth, and sector width. OPEN IN VIEWER Table 1: Scanning Parameters and Techniques Affecting CEUS Study Quality Output Power.—The amplitude of the transmitted pulse, referred to as output power and related to the MI, must be low enough during contrast-enhanced imaging to minimize bubble destruction while maintaining an adequate signal-to-noise ratio (14). US machine vendors provide standard output power settings that are optimized for most scenarios, which generally do not require adjusting. In select situations (deep lesions, obese patients, hepatic steatosis), it may be appropriate to increase the output power to achieve additional penetration (2,14,15). Although increasing the output power will result in increased backscatter signal from microbubbles, it may also increase image noise and accentuate some artifacts such as pseudoenhancement. (See the “Bubble Destruction” section.) Increasing output power also accelerates microbubble destruction, decreases the contrast agent half-life, and may cause pseudowashout (2,15). If the output power is too low, the contrast mode image will be too dark. Image Gain.—Image gain, a postprocessing parameter, reflects the overall amplification of returning echoes and affects image brightness. When the contrast-enhanced image gain is correctly adjusted, the organ or tissue of interest is black or nearly black, leaving only a few highly echogenic interfaces visible. When the image gain is set too high, the precontrast image shows diffuse bright pixels, indicating noise (Fig 6) (14). When the gain is set too low, the contrast-enhanced image may be too dark for interpretation. Therefore, it is recommended that highly echogenic interfaces, such as fascial planes, organ capsules, vessel walls, bowel gas, and calcifications, remain visible (Fig 6). As it is difficult to eliminate all echoes from the contrast-only image before contrast agent administration, careful comparison of the precontrast versus postcontrast images may be necessary to ensure that a signal seen on the postinjection postcontrast image is from microbubbles and not due to artifact. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 6. Importance of precontrast gain settings demonstrated in a 57-year-old man with a left renal mass and widespread metastatic disease who presented for biopsy. (A–C) Sequential dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images were obtained through the left renal mass before contrast agent administration. With a correctly optimized image gain (A), echogenic interfaces such as fascial planes (arrows) and calcifications (arrowheads) should remain bright. With the gain set too low (B), noise is amplified, producing a diffuse haze over the entire image. With the gain set too high (C), noise is amplified, producing a diffuse haze over the entire image. (D) Biopsy planning image 36 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres shows near-complete nonenhancement in the center of the mass (*) and along the path of the planned needle placement (dashed line), while the nodular enhancing tissue is seen along the periphery (arrows). The biopsy needle trajectory was changed and viable tissue was obtained. Download as PowerPoint TEACHING POINT The image gain should be adjusted before UCA administration. Adjusting the gain after UCA administration is generally not recommended. Although increasing the gain can result in increased brightness from the microbubbles, it also amplifies noise, and differentiating enhancing from nonenhancing structures may be difficult (2,15). If quantitative perfusion measurements are needed, the contrast presets should not be adjusted, as this may alter the input signal and lead to errors in time-intensity curve analysis (16). Similar to overall gain adjustments, time-gain-compensation adjustments should be made before the contrast agent administration to achieve image uniformity from the near field to the far field (Fig 7). Fine adjustments can be made to compensate for excessive attenuation by amplifying the signal from the far field, although these adjustments should be made with caution, given the risk of amplifying noise and pseudoenhancement. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 7. Multiple complex-appearing hepatic cysts in a 50-year-old man. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the left liver lobe show a cyst (*) with posterior acoustic enhancement (arrowheads). The dark horizontal band (arrows) across the contrast-only image indicates inappropriately adjusted time-gain-compensation in the midfield. Download as PowerPoint Frame Rate.—The frame rate is the number of images displayed per unit of time, expressed in hertz. The frame rate generally is automatically set to the maximal value that is allowable, depending on the sector width, image depth, and other settings. Some systems allow a user to decrease the frame rate (sometimes referred to as the pulse repetition frequency). Although high frame rates allow higher temporal resolution, they may also lead to accelerated microbubble destruction (2). In contrast mode, frame rates may be reduced owing to the multipulse techniques used for contrast-enhanced imaging. Reducing the frame rate further may minimize microbubble destruction, and this may be helpful in evaluating lesions with slow washout, such as some hepatocellular carcinomas. Conversely, rapidly enhancing lesions may need a higher frame rate for adequate assessment of high-flow structures or lesions (17). Focal Zone.—Ultrasound energy deposition and, thus, microbubble disruption, is not uniform throughout the image. Much of the ultrasound beam energy is concentrated at the focal zone. To minimize microbubble destruction, the focal zone should generally be positioned well beyond (deep to) the target. Many users place the focal zone at the lowest depth in the field of view. When assessing focal liver lesions, it may be appropriate to place the focal zone just below the lesion of interest to balance spatial resolution and microbubble destruction (17). Transducer Frequency.—Optimal microbubble resonance is achieved at lower frequencies (1–3 MHz). Although imaging at lower frequencies improves penetration, it negatively impacts spatial resolution. Higher-frequency curvilinear transducers and high-frequency linear transducers may be used for CEUS to improve spatial resolution; however, the signal from microbubbles may not be as strong, resulting in an overall darker contrast-enhanced image (Fig 8). Also, at higher frequencies, a smaller distribution of microbubble sizes will be stimulated. For these reasons, a higher contrast agent dose—up to 1.5 to two times the dose of that for the liver or kidney—may be needed. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 8. Effect of transmit frequency on spatial resolution and contrast sensitivity demonstrated in a 65-year-old man with a history of prostate cancer and an incidental testicular lesion. (A–C) Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the left testis after intravenous administration of 2.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres, obtained by using various linear transducers, show a hypoechoic solid-appearing intraparenchymal testicular mass (arrows). Images obtained 31 seconds after contrast agent administration by using a 6–18-MHz linear transducer (A) show the mass with solid enhancement, as well as a subtle adjacent feeding artery (arrowhead in A) with high spatial resolution. On the contrast-only images obtained at 49 seconds by using a 5–14-MHz linear transducer (B) and at 1 minute 44 seconds by using a 4–10-MHz linear transducer (C), the enhancement appears to be more robust, and more microbubbles are detected throughout the parenchyma. With each subsequent decrease in transmit frequency, the signal from the microbubbles improves, but at the expense of worsening spatial resolution. Download as PowerPoint Preparing for Success Before performing CEUS, it is important to optimize the patient positioning and transducer orientation to maximize visualization of the region of interest during the examination. The following are a few tips to achieve these goals. Ergonomics.—Before the UCA is injected, both the sonographer and the patient should be in a comfortable position that can be maintained for the duration of the examination. As imaging may last for several minutes and several injections may be attempted, the patient needs to be in a comfortable position to minimize movement, which may alter the acoustic window. Similarly, as a specific orientation will be maintained for a prolonged period, the sonographer should ensure that their body, arm, and hand are supported and relaxed to minimize cramping and body strain. Depth and Patient Positioning.—Depth is generally a limiting factor in B-mode imaging, CEUS in particular. Loss of signal in the far field is intrinsic to US and occurs owing to attenuation of the ultrasound pulse. Given the low–output-power (ie, low-MI) mode required for contrast-enhanced imaging, lesions deeper than 10 cm can be challenging to image. If a lesion is not well seen on gray-scale images, visualizing it in contrast mode will be difficult (Fig 9). To best visualize the contrast signal response, it is important to decrease the distance between the area of interest and the transducer, often by adjusting the patient’s position or changing the transducer orientation to demonstrate the target within a depth of 10 cm. Several patient positions and acoustic windows from various directions (lateral, posterior, or intercostal) should be attempted to maximize visualization while minimizing the target-to-transducer distance (14,18). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 9. Change in patient positioning to decrease depth and improve target lesion visualization in a 52-year-old woman with an incidental liver lesion identified at US performed for elevated liver function test results. (A) B-mode US image through the right liver lobe shows an echogenic nodule (arrows) in the deep posterior aspect of the right lobe near the dome. (B) Corresponding dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images of the lesion (arrows) obtained using the same approach as in A, 43 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres, show a lack of signal in the far field of the liver due to attenuation and poor acoustic penetration. The lesion was nonevaluable. (C) After repositioning the patient and imaging from a posterolateral approach, the distance to the target lesion (arrows) was significantly reduced. After a second injection, peripheral discontinuous nodular enhancement (arrowheads), with subsequent centripetal enhancement and lack of washout (not shown), on the contrast-only image was diagnostic for hemangioma. Download as PowerPoint Decreasing the transducer transmit frequency may improve visualization of deeper structures, but it negatively impacts spatial resolution. If additional signal from deeper structures is needed or in livers that are difficult to image (such as in cirrhosis or steatosis), the output power can be increased with caution (17). Acoustic Window.—TEACHING POINT An acoustic window that allows visualization of the target with the least amount of depth and minimal shadowing from gas or ribs is ideal. When possible, the target should be imaged in a plane (typically sagittal) that keeps the area of interest in the field of view during quiet respiration and enables visualization of the adjacent normal parenchyma for comparison. Ideally, one should avoid using the liver or spleen as an acoustic window (eg, when evaluating a renal lesion), as the overlying enhancing parenchyma may lead to accentuated attenuation and some forms of pseudoenhancement. (See the “Pseudoenhancement” and “Attenuation and Shadowing” sections.) Contrast Agent Dose.—Contemporary ultrasound devices and software generally offer higher sensitivity to UCAs than older-generation systems. Therefore, the doses of intravenously injected contrast agents are generally lower than the doses of contrast media listed in U.S. Food and Drug Administraton documents and on package inserts. The contrast agent dose may vary, depending on the organ or tissue of interest, with vascular applications and organs with large relative blood volume (such as the liver and kidneys) requiring smaller doses than targets with lower relative blood volume (eg, testes). As microbubbles are highly reflective, excessive concentrations may lead to undesirable attenuation and even acoustic shadowing, particularly in intracavitary applications (Fig 10). Dose-related effects on image quality are detailed in the “Artifacts from Microbubbles” section. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 10. Serial concentrations of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres diluted in 500 mL of water (with contrast agent doses and concentrations listed) show optimal homogeneous enhancement between concentrations of 0.01% and 0.2%. At concentrations of 0.4% and higher, microbubbles result in increasing attenuation of the incident ultrasound beam. Ultimately, at the highest concentration (1.0%), microbubbles result in shadowing of the deeper contents of the container. Download as PowerPoint For subsequent injections, waiting for complete clearance of previously injected microbubbles is recommended, particularly if perfusion analysis is desired. To shorten the contrast agent half-life and the wait time between injections, standard B-mode imaging, color Doppler US, or the scanner’s burst, or flash, pulse sequence can be used to rapidly destroy bubbles. It is important to note that microbubble destruction results in high mechanical energy deposition, so users should follow the ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable) principles when imaging potentially sensitive tissue (7,8).

Image Artifacts An imaging artifact is an image finding or feature that does not accurately represent the object being imaged (19,20). Artifacts are routinely encountered on medical images of any modality. US image artifacts may be due to improper technique or an unexpected interaction within the object being imaged or may be intrinsic to the modality (eg, processing error) (21). Many US artifacts are unwanted and can obscure anatomy, degrade image quality, or simulate disease, leading to confusion and errors in image interpretation. However, some US artifacts are useful in making certain diagnoses or can provide information related to the composition of the structure or tissue being imaged (eg, increased through- transmission or posterior acoustic enhancement of cysts, acoustic shadowing of stones) (20). CEUS artifacts may be related to common B-mode artifacts, specific to contrast-enhanced imaging techniques, or related to the microbubbles themselves. In the remainder of this article, we review many of these artifacts, with an emphasis on their imaging appearances and the strategies to minimize their impact. Standard B-mode Artifacts on CEUS Images For B-mode image formation, ultrasound equipment relies on specific physical assumptions to assign the location and intensity of each echo (19,20). In clinical scanning, when assumptions regarding ultrasound beam characteristics, echo path, speed of sound, and attenuation are violated, various artifacts may become apparent on the gray-scale image (19,20). The common artifacts seen with B-mode US, including posterior acoustic shadowing and enhancement; signal attenuation, refraction, and reflection; and reverberation, are often apparent and may be accentuated at CEUS (Table 2). OPEN IN VIEWER Table 2: Common US Artifacts on CEUS Images Posterior Acoustic Shadowing and Enhancement.—Posterior acoustic shadowing results from highly attenuating and/or reflective structures such as stones, calcified atherosclerotic plaque, and air-filled bowel, resulting in absent backscatter signal from deeper structures. Although this is commonly seen on the gray-scale image, shadowing may appear accentuated at CEUS as the output power is decreased, and spatial compounding is deactivated in the contrast mode (Fig 11). Shadowing may obscure the target area of interest, in which case changes in acoustic window or altering the patient position may be needed. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 11. Posterior acoustic shadowing on contrast mode US images in a 76-year-old man with a renal mass that was found incidentally during the workup for suspected hydronephrosis. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the left kidney 25 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show a solid enhancing exophytic mass (solid arrows) arising from the left kidney (*). A large calcification (open arrows) in the center of the mass results in shadowing of deeper structures (arrowheads). Download as PowerPoint Posterior acoustic enhancement (ie, increased through- transmission) occurs when weakly attenuating structures with minimal contrast enhancement (classically cysts) show increased amplitude of deeper echoes (Fig 12). Acoustic enhancement may also be seen on CEUS images; however, corrective action is typically not needed. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 12. Posterior acoustic enhancement on contrast mode US images in a 52-year-old woman with multiple liver lesions who underwent renal transplantation evaluation. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe 50 seconds after intravenous administration of 2.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show increased signal from the liver parenchyma (arrowheads) deep to a simple nonenhancing cyst (*). Download as PowerPoint Signal Attenuation.—Ultrasound beam attenuation on contrast-enhanced images appears as progressive loss of microbubble signal in the far field (Fig 13). The contrast mode is a low-output-power (low-MI) mode, which impacts the acoustic energy of returning echoes. Although the output power can be increased, this comes at the expense of increased microbubble destruction, particularly in the near field. Alternatively, a lower transmit frequency transducer may improve the signal from the far field. As previously mentioned, repositioning the patient to decrease the distance between the transducer and the region of interest may diminish the impact of attenuation (Fig 9). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 13. Signal attenuation on US images in a 49-year-old woman who underwent evaluation for an incidental liver lesion. (A) Longitudinal gray-scale image of the right liver lobe shows an echogenic severely attenuating liver and loss of echogenicity of the vessel walls and diaphragm—findings of moderate to severe steatosis. (B) Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode images 41 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show loss of signal in the far field, despite the use of a very-low-frequency transducer. Download as PowerPoint Refraction and Reflection.—Refraction and reflection artifacts manifest owing to the assumption that ultrasound pulses travel in a straight line to and from a target. Refraction artifacts are typically seen as bands of low signal extending vertically along the axial scanning direction, with the apex at the margin of a curved interface separating two tissues (“edge shadow”). If the target area of interest is obscured by refractive shadowing, changes in the acoustic window or patient repositioning may change the angle of incidence enough to minimize or eliminate this artifact. Reflection artifact also occurs owing to the error in assuming that an ultrasound pulse propagates in a single straight path. A classic mirror image artifact is caused by a highly reflective curved surface, such as the diaphragm, that acts as a mirror (Fig 14). This artifact is generally not a diagnostic dilemma, as it is typically sufficient to recognize and disregard the duplicated structure, often mapped in the chest cavity. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 14. Mirror image artifact on contrast mode US images in a 37-year-old woman with multiple liver metastases with rapid washout (arrows). Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe 1 minute 34 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show a liver metastasis near the dome, with an identical-appearing finding: a mirror artifact (arrowheads) of the adjacent liver lesion, mapped on the opposite side of the diaphragm. Download as PowerPoint Reverberation.—Reverberation results in image clutter in the near field, or sequential equally spaced bright lines deep to a pair of echogenic interfaces (Fig 15). This artifact originates from the assumption that there is only a single reflection per ultrasound pulse. In practice, there are many internal reflections, as returning echoes may encounter additional interfaces that further split the acoustic energy into transmitted and re-reflected components. If an ultrasound pulse encounters two relatively closely spaced echogenic interfaces, multiple internal reflections will result in echoes returning later and thus being mapped in the image deeper than the physical location of the structure. For multiple closely apposed interfaces, such as those in the anterior abdominal wall, the consolidation of echoes may result in diffuse image clutter in the near field (Fig 15). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 15. Reverberation artifact on contrast mode US images in a 78-year-old woman with a cystic renal mass. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the left kidney 36 seconds after intravenous administration of 0.2 mL of perflutren lipid microspheres (Definity, Lantheus) show a partially exophytic renal cyst (*) with a thin septum (arrowheads). Linear echogenic bands (solid arrows) in the near field of the cyst represent reverberation artifact from the anterior abdominal wall. Adjacent noise (open arrows) in the near field of the cyst likely represents a combination of reverberation artifact and nonlinear artifacts from the anterior abdominal wall. Download as PowerPoint Other commonly encountered B-mode artifacts, such as those caused by side lobes and elevation plane volume averaging, can also be seen at CEUS, but these are not described in this article.

Contrast Mode–specific Artifacts Several artifacts are related to the specific techniques used for CEUS (Table 3). OPEN IN VIEWER Table 3: CEUS-specific Artifacts Suppression and Nonlinear Artifacts The tissue subtraction (suppression of signal from background tissues) achieved by using the contrast mode is highly effective for homogeneous tissues. As discussed earlier, some structures, interfaces, and tissues may remain echogenic on the contrast-only image obtained before contrast agent administration, despite appropriate image gain. This is most apparent at highly echogenic interfaces such as abdominal wall fascial planes, organ capsules, calcifications, bowel lumens, and vessel walls (Fig 6). These artifacts can usually be identified before the injection and have a correlate structure on the corresponding gray-scale image. This is of particular importance immediately following percutaneous ablation, when large bubbles within the ablation bed may remain apparent on the contrast-only image (Fig 16). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 16. Nonlinear artifacts from echogenic interfaces on contrast mode US images in a 68-year-old woman who had hepatocellular carcinoma, with a new liver mass, and underwent microwave ablation. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe were obtained immediately following the ablation, 18 seconds after intravenous administration of 0.4 mL of perflutren lipid microspheres. The echogenic ablation site (arrows) on the gray-scale image is seen as a geographic region devoid of enhancement on the contrast-only image. On the contrast-only image, foci of high signal from large bubbles (arrowhead on both images) located centrally in the otherwise devascularized ablation cavity are seen. The adjacent gallbladder (*) contains shadowing gallstones. Download as PowerPoint Although most tissues respond linearly to an ultrasound pulse and are easily nulled on the contrast-only image, a smaller component of the response may be nonlinear, resulting in incomplete suppression. Phase aberrations that occur in some tissues will result in less effective subtraction and are most commonly seen in the subcutaneous adipose tissue and steatotic liver (Fig 15). Ultrasound pulses propagating through a large number of microbubbles result in a nonlinear artifactual signal from deeper structures, leading to pseudoenhancement. (See the “Pseudoenhancement” section.) If differentiating truly enhancing tissues from pseudoenhancement remains a diagnostic dilemma, the burst, or flash, feature can be used to purposely destroy the microbubble-related signal within the field of view. For truly enhancing tissues, the signal will be immediately lost and reperfusion may be seen, whereas artifactual signal will have a similar appearance before and after microbubble destruction (Fig 17). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 17. Focused microbubble destruction to differentiate artifact from true enhancement on dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images in a 58-year-old man with acute-on-chronic kidney disease and an indeterminate cystic mass (arrows). (A) Images 56 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show the left kidney (*) and a partially exophytic cyst with an echogenic septum (arrowheads). (B) Images obtained with the ultrasound system’s burst, or flash, function activated show a resultant marked increase in signal throughout the contrast-only image. (C) On images obtained immediately after B, the majority of the signal throughout the kidney is lost, with the septum (arrowheads) remaining echogenic, indicating that the bright signal is not from the microbubbles but rather is artifactual. Download as PowerPoint Bubble Destruction TEACHING POINT Unexpected loss of the contrast signal may be the result of unintentional microbubble destruction. Even with a microbubble-specific low-MI imaging mode, a small proportion of microbubbles may be disrupted with each incident pulse, and this destruction may not be uniform throughout the target or tissue. Continuous imaging, higher frame rates, and higher output power will exacerbate the artifacts discussed in the sections that follow. To preserve microbubbles (ie, increase the contrast agent half-life) and minimize some of these artifacts, brief intervals of viewing and imaging (every 30–60 seconds after the arterial phase) can be interleaved with periods of nonimaging when the image is “frozen”; this technique is called intermittent imaging (2). Returning to the standard B-mode or initiating color Doppler US will rapidly destroy microbubbles and should be avoided if contrast preservation is desired. Near-Field Signal Loss.—Near-field signal loss manifests as a progressive asymmetric decrease in the microbubble-related signal within the tissues closest to the transducer, leading to a dark horizontal band in the near field (Fig 18). Acoustic energy absorption is not uniform within the field of view and is greatest in the superficial tissues and at the focal zone. This asymmetric energy deposition, often called bubble “burnoff,” results in greater microbubble destruction in the near field (17) and is accentuated with a higher MI, with higher scanning frequencies, and at higher frame rates. Corrective measures include use of lower frame rates, intermittent imaging, and frequent changes in transducer orientation. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 18. Near-field microbubble burnoff on contrast mode US images in an 88-year-old man undergoing evaluation of an incidental liver lesion (not shown). Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe 4 minutes 52 seconds after injection of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show a band of low signal (arrows) within the parenchyma in the near field due to accelerated microbubble destruction. Download as PowerPoint Imaging Plane Signal Loss.—In-plane signal loss is seen as diffuse signal loss in large areas of imaged tissue and is often recognized when sweeping through a region previously imaged or changing the transducer orientation to an orthogonal plane in which a band of low signal will be evident (Fig 19, Movie 2). This artifact results from accelerated microbubble destruction in the imaging plane relative to adjacent out-of-plane tissues and is accentuated when the transducer remains in a single location for a prolonged period, leading to a population of microbubbles receiving continuous insonation. This phenomenon may be mitigated by ensuring low-MI imaging, decreasing the frame rate, leveraging intermittent imaging, and sweeping through an area instead of remaining in one fixed location (14,17,22,23). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 19. In-plane microbubble burnoff on contrast mode US images in a 52-year-old woman undergoing evaluation of renal cysts. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe, oriented orthogonal to the previous image plane and obtained 6 minutes 22 seconds after injection of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres, show a vertical band of low signal (arrows) within the liver parenchyma, indicating accelerated microbubble destruction from prolonged imaging of the kidney while using the liver as an acoustic window. Download as PowerPoint Pseudowashout.—The asymmetric loss of contrast signal may also result from nonuniform destruction of microbubbles between tissues—that is, pseudowashout—as opposed to the true washout seen in lesions with distinctly different perfusion characteristics (Fig E1) (24,25). Pseudowashout can be seen in slow-flow lesions such as hemangiomas (Fig 20). If microbubbles are replenished at a slower rate or destroyed at a higher rate, this may lead to alterations in the appearance of the washout. This phenomenon is of particular importance in liver imaging, when washout is one characteristic that is critical for lesion characterization (26,27). Pseudowashout may then lead to diagnostic dilemmas or even misdiagnoses. Again, decreasing the frame rate, leveraging intermittent imaging, making sure that the focal zone is beyond the area of interest, and sweeping through a lesion instead of prolonged continuous stationary imaging may help eliminate this artifact. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 20. Pseudowashout on contrast mode US images in a 44-year-old man with an incidental liver lesion. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the left liver lobe show a hypoechoic mass (arrows) within an echogenic steatotic liver on the gray-scale images. (A) Images 14 seconds after injection of 0.5 mL of perflutren lipid microspheres show the nodule with a spoke-wheel pattern of arterial phase hyperenhancement (arrowheads), with subsequent centrifugal fill-in (not shown), consistent with focal nodular hyperplasia. (B) Images obtained after 3 minutes 21 seconds of continuous imaging show apparent washout from the nodule (arrowheads), presumably due to accelerated bubble destruction from the continuous imaging focused on the target nodule. Download as PowerPoint Artifacts from Microbubbles Finally, some artifacts are related directly to the microbubbles themselves (Table 4). OPEN IN VIEWER Table 4: Artifacts from Microbubbles Pseudoenhancement.—Similar to phase aberrations accumulated by propagating ultrasound pulses traveling through fatty tissues, phase aberrations in CEUS result from a large population of microbubbles superficial to a target. This may manifest as hypoenhancing or nonenhancing echogenic structures or tissues showing an artifactual increase in signal after contrast agent administration (pseudoenhancement) (2,28). This form of pseudoenhancement is not apparent before the injection of microbubbles, differentiating it from incompletely suppressed echogenic interfaces that appear the same before and following contrast agent administration (Fig 21). This artifact is most commonly seen when imaging through the liver or spleen and worsens with increasing bubble concentration and increasing image gain or output power. To minimize this artifact, decrease the skin-to-target distance and attempt to use an acoustic window that minimizes or eliminates intervening, highly enhancing tissue between the transducer and target (Fig 21). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 21. Pseudoenhancement from phase aberrations on dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the upper pole of the left kidney in a 37-year-old man with abdominal pain and an incidental renal mass (arrows). (A) Images before contrast agent administration show a peripherally calcified exophytic renal mass on the gray-scale image, with relatively little corresponding echogenicity on the contrast-only image. (B) Images 24 seconds after intravenous injection of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show the peripherally calcified wall of the mass on the gray-scale image and several calcified septa (arrowheads) with increased signal, brighter than that of the adjacent kidney, on the contrast-only image. This signal did not move, although it slowly decreased as contrast material cleared from the overlying spleen, indicating pseudoenhancement. (C) The patient was repositioned and a posterior approach without an intervening spleen was chosen. Images obtained after administration of an additional contrast agent dose show that on the contrast-only image, much of the calcified wall remain nonechogenic, but enhancing irregular septa and mural nodules become apparent (arrowheads), indicating a cystic renal mass. Download as PowerPoint Pseudoenhancement is particularly troublesome following ablation (such as radiofrequency or microwave ablation), when highly echogenic material may remain within the ablation bed (Fig 22). Nonlinear artifact may manifest as a nonmarginal (central) signal within the ablation zone that increases after contrast agent administration and that can be mistaken for enhancement. However, true enhancement from residual or recurrent viable disease typically appears at the periphery during the arterial phase, instead of centrally, and often later in the portal venous phase, as with pseudoenhancement. Pseudoenhancement may be minimized by choosing the appropriate power and gain settings before the contrast material injection and reducing the dose of the injected agent. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 22. Pseudoenhancement from phase aberrations on contrast mode US images in a 66-year-old man with a history of ablation for hepatocellular carcinoma. (A) Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe before contrast agent administration show an ill-defined hyperechoic nodule (arrows) on the gray-scale image, with no corresponding echogenicity on the contrast-only image. (B) Corresponding images 20 seconds after intravenous injection of 1.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show the ablation cavity on the contrast-only image (arrows), with apparent enhancement within the center of the cavity, corresponding to the most echogenic component on the corresponding gray-scale image (arrowheads). Recurrence after ablation is expected to be along the margin of the ablation zone, not centrally. This signal did not move, although it slowly decreased as contrast material cleared from the overlying liver, indicating pseudoenhancement. Download as PowerPoint Finally, if the gain or output power is inappropriately increased in an attempt to improve the signal from the far field or increase the signal from microbubbles in the late phase after the contrast agent injection, noise and any incompletely suppressed signal from phase aberrations may lead to pseudoenhancement (Fig 23) (26). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 23. Pseudoenhancement and signal saturation due to a high contrast agent dose and inappropriately high image gain on contrast mode US images in a 39-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease and an enlarging renal mass. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images 14 seconds after intravenous injection of 2.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show signal, misinterpreted as enhancement, within the mass (solid arrows). The gray-scale image through the atrophic right kidney (*) in long orientation shows a mildly heterogeneous, hypoechoic, partially exophytic mass in the interpolar region. Remote CT (not shown) showed a simple cyst, while follow-up MRI (not shown) showed a progressive decrease in mass size and findings of blood products, suggesting a cyst complicated by hemorrhage. At CEUS, the signal in the renal mass was found to be pseudoenhancement from an inappropriately high image gain and probably an unnecessarily high contrast agent dose. These findings were supported by the exceptionally bright areas within the liver and kidney (arrowheads), mapped to the highest value on the adjacent color scale (open arrow) and possibly indicating signal saturation, that are most commonly seen with inappropriately high image gain, output power, and contrast agent dose. Download as PowerPoint Signal Saturation.—Signal saturation occurs when the signal intensity of echoes overpowers the scanner’s maximal allowable backscatter signal and is higher than is allowed on the display. After contrast agent administration, some highly enhancing structures may appear nearly white on the contrast-only image, mapped to color values at the top of the scale (Fig 23) (29,30). The causes of this phenomenon include high output power or a high dose of UCA. Signal saturation occurs more commonly in renal transplantation or angiographic studies owing to the relatively high concentration of microbubbles or when imaging highly vascular lesions. A uniformly intense signal throughout a perfused organ or structure can mask underlying subtle changes of early enhancement or washout and confound the interpretation of contrast kinetics at time-intensity curve analysis. The time-intensity curve misinterpretation occurs when the highest possible pixel value, displayed as a plateau, is reached, and, thus, the true peak enhancement may not be detectable. Strategies to mitigate this artifact include decreasing the contrast agent dose and decreasing the output power, although the latter approach should be used with caution when attempting perfusion quantification. Attenuation and Shadowing.—TEACHING POINT The attenuation and shadowing seen on gray-scale images also appear on contrast-enhanced images. Microbubbles also absorb and reflect acoustic energy, and a high concentration of microbubbles in the near field can result in accentuated attenuation, leading to poor visualization of deeper structures (Fig 10) (2). This is commonly encountered in highly perfused organs, such as the liver, resulting in a markedly attenuated signal from the right kidney (Fig 24). Waiting for clearance of microbubbles from the near field or decreasing the contrast material dose at a subsequent injection may be helpful. An alternative acoustic window that minimizes intervening structures between the transducer and target can also be attempted. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 24. Attenuation from contrast agent on contrast mode US images in a 52-year-old woman with an incidental liver lesion found to be a cyst (not shown). Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the right liver lobe after intravenous injection of 2.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres show marked loss of signal (arrowheads), Which is more apparent on the contrast-only image, from the deepest aspects of the liver. Attenuation is affected by microbubbles in the more superficial parts of the liver, resulting in accentuated attenuation of sound pulses as they propagate to and return from the deeper parts of the liver. Download as PowerPoint The reflection and absorption effect of a high concentration of microbubbles can be so great as to lead to posterior acoustic shadowing (Fig 10). This is most commonly encountered with intracavitary contrast agent injections such as those in contrast-enhanced vesicourosonography or antegrade pyelography (Fig 25) (31,32). Care must be taken to appropriately dilute the UCA (typically with normal saline) to prevent excessive attenuation and shadowing in these applications. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 25. (A) Shadowing from a high concentration of microbubbles on contrast mode US images in a 39-year-old man with a history of nephrolithiasis who underwent CEUS antegrade pyelography following percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) to confirm ureteral patency. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images through the bladder after injection of 1.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres via an indwelling nephrostomy tube show a contrast agent–filled bladder (*), although the high concentration of microbubbles resulted in posterior acoustic shadowing (arrowheads). (B) Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) images from antegrade pyelography in a 55-year-old man following PCNL with injection of a lower dose of contrast agent (0.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres) via an indwelling nephrostomy tube show less shadowing than that seen in A, with a homogeneous appearance of the bladder (*) and visualization of the Foley catheter balloon (black arrow) and the back wall (white arrows) of the bladder. Download as PowerPoint Color Doppler Artifacts.—The presence of microbubbles in the circulation can affect the color Doppler signal from vessels. The marked increase in backscatter signal from the microbubbles significantly increases the detection of Doppler shifts, resulting in an increase in color Doppler signal to a degree such that color is mapped beyond the physical vessel wall (“blooming” artifact), wherein the color appears to “bleed” out from the vessel lumen (Fig 26) (17,33,34). Correction can be achieved by increasing the Doppler scale and decreasing the Doppler gain. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 26. Color Doppler blooming artifact from circulating microbubbles in a 78-year-old man undergoing evaluation for an incidental liver lesion (not shown). (A) Color Doppler US image through the right liver lobe shows normal color fill-in of the main portal vein (arrows). (B) Color Doppler US image less than 2 minutes after intravenous administration of 1.0 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres shows a marked increase in the color Doppler signal (arrows) despite identical Doppler scale and gain settings, with color overwriting structures outside the vessel wall (blooming). In addition, scattered foci of various colors (arrowheads), appearing as a mosaic pattern of color, are seen in the adjacent liver parenchyma as a result of microbubble destruction and deconvolution errors in Doppler image processing. Download as PowerPoint As the color Doppler mode is a relatively high-MI mode, rapid microbubble destruction will occur when this mode is activated. This rapid destruction of microbubbles within the parenchyma of an organ may be misinterpreted as the Doppler shifts, and a mosaic of color combinations is seen diffusely throughout the parenchyma (Fig 26). This finding generally resolves within a few seconds of scanning. Pulse-Wave Doppler Artifacts.—Artifact from intravascular gas has a characteristic appearance on pulse-wave Doppler US images. The classic example is that of gas bubbles in the portal vein that result in high-amplitude or high-intensity spikes superimposed on the normal portal vein spectral Doppler waveform tracing. In a similar manner, if spectral Doppler imaging is attempted shortly after UCA injection, these high-intensity spikes may be superimposed on spectral waveforms, simulating portal venous gas (Fig 27) (2,34). In addition, an increase in the overall Doppler signal may overcome the filters designed to reduce noise (21). These phenomena may lead to an error in estimating the correct peak systolic velocity, especially if automatic calculation software is used (35). It is recommended that any needed Doppler US examination be performed before UCA administration. Otherwise, the sonographer will need to wait a sufficient amount of time for complete microbubble clearance (at least three half-lives, or 10–15 minutes). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 27. Spectral Doppler US high-intensity transient spikes from microbubbles in a 46-year-old man who underwent en bloc renal transplantation, now presenting with acute kidney injury. Duplex color (top) and spectral Doppler (bottom) US image of the artery of the medial transplant moiety after intravenous administration of 0.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres shows randomly distributed high-intensity spikes of signal above and below the baseline (arrowheads) resulting from resonance and destruction of microbubbles as they pass through the pulse-wave Doppler sample line, impacting the ability to assess the waveform and choose an appropriate peak velocity. Download as PowerPoint Persistent Heterogeneous Liver Enhancement.—Adverse events following UCA administration are exceedingly rare. One rare phenomenon, known as persistent heterogeneous liver enhancement (PHLE), manifests as markedly heterogeneous echogenic liver parenchyma that appears cloudy or wool-like on gray-scale images, and as patchy echogenicity on contrast-enhanced images (Fig 28) (36,37). This finding may appear shortly after UCA administration and can last for several hours, slowly resolving over the subsequent 1–2 days. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 28. Persistent heterogeneous liver enhancement on US images in a 71-year-old man with cirrhosis and proctocolitis who underwent liver US owing to decompensation following echocardiography, during which a “bubble study” was performed. Gray-scale images through the right (A) and left (B) liver lobes show a small cirrhotic liver and large right pleural effusion. The hepatic parenchyma contains heterogeneous irregular bands of marked echogenicity (arrowheads) that was first interpreted as embolized portal venous gas. Subsequent CT (not shown) revealed no evidence of bowel compromise. The bands of echogenicity subsequently resolved and were attributed to persistent heterogeneous liver enhancement from the previously administered UCA used for echocardiography. Download as PowerPoint PHLE has been reported more commonly in young thin patients who received multiple or large contrast agent doses, and there appears to be a correlation with enteritis and colitis (36,37). The cause of PHLE is unknown, although hypotheses include fusion of multiple microbubbles and insufflation and expansion of circulating microbubbles due to bowel gas from an inflamed bowel, resulting in embolization of the macrobubbles into the liver (38–40). These hypotheses are supported by the finding that these large bubbles are generally seen only in the portal circulation, are seen on both contrast-only and gray-scale images, and are not destroyed by high-MI imaging techniques. The imaging appearance may raise concern, given the features that overlap with those of portal venous gas in necrotizing enterocolitis. However, several retrospective reviews (36,38–40) have shown no adverse outcomes, and patients universally remain asymptomatic.

Conclusion All imaging modalities have artifacts that may negatively impact examination quality and diagnostic confidence. Understanding the technical principles behind these artifacts allows users to modify their scanning technique to minimize many of these artifacts. For interpreters of CEUS studies, identifying and possibly correcting these artifacts facilitate higher image quality, improved diagnostic accuracy, and better patient care.
rawBodyText:
Introduction The introduction of ultrasound-specific contrast agents (UCAs) has led to a renaissance in US, allowing new imaging and interventional techniques that previously were not possible with US. Contrast-enhanced US (CEUS) leverages the inherent benefits of a portable, widely available, and cost-effective modality with UCAs that offer exceptional safety and other unique benefits compared with contrast agents used in CT and MRI (1). CEUS has been used worldwide for years, with several U.S. Food and Drug Administration–approved UCAs in the United States and reimbursement billing codes now available. With the increasing use of CEUS, it is important that radiologists recognize artifacts unique to this modality to help improve image quality, avoid misinterpretations, and develop comfort and confidence in performing the technique (2). Microbubble Formulation and Pharmacokinetics UCAs consist of small particles that are approximately the size of red blood cells (3–5 µm) (3). These particles, commonly known as microbubbles, are composed of an inert, high-molecular-weight, poorly dissolvable fluorocarbon gas core encapsulated in a biocompatible lipid or protein shell. The gas is excreted by means of exhalation, while the phospholipids or proteins of the shell are processed by the reticuloendothelial system. Microbubble clearance is rapid, with a half-life of several minutes; this allows multiple injections during a single examination, providing the opportunity to image multiple organs and characterize multiple lesions (4–8). Subsequent contrast agent doses can generally be administered after two to three half-lives (or about 10 minutes). Purposeful microbubble destruction can accelerate contrast agent clearance. Although U.S. Food and Drug Administration dose limits exist, given their short half-life, higher total administered doses can be used off-label. TEACHING POINT Microbubbles are too large to pass through the vessel endothelium and are purely intravascular contrast agents. Unlike iodinated and gadolinium-based contrast agents that diffuse out of the vascular space, leading to delayed extravascular interstitial accumulation and enhancement, microbubbles function as a pure blood pool agent (Fig 1) (9). This quality allows real-time angiographic imaging of vessels and perfused tissues at high temporal and spatial resolution. UCAs may also be injected into other cavities and spaces and typically will not intravasate into the blood pool. UCAs are not excreted by the kidneys, so urographic imaging may be achieved only by means of direct injection into the urinary tract. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 1. Diagram illustrating intravenously injected radiologic contrast agents shows iodinated and gadolinium molecules exiting the vascular space and entering the extravascular interstitial space, while microbubble particles, like red blood cells, are too large and remain within the vascular space, functioning as a pure blood pool agent. Download as PowerPoint Microbubble-Ultrasound Interaction An ultrasound pulse is a mechanical wave composed of compression and rarefaction sequences at frequencies in the range of 1–20 MHz. Microbubbles contract and expand (ie, oscillate) when exposed to ultrasound at specific frequencies (resonance), usually 1–3 MHz. The degree of oscillation is dependent on the peak pressure. At pressures lower than 100 kPa, microbubbles demonstrate symmetric contraction and rarefaction, also known as linear oscillation (Fig 2). Between peak pressures of 100 and 500 kPa, there is a nonlinear response, meaning the degree of rarefaction is out of proportion to the degree of compression, resulting in a several-fold change in the diameter of a microbubble (10–12). This nonlinear oscillation generates strong harmonic and ultraharmonic frequencies in higher and lower multiples of the transmitted (fundamental) frequency. Most tissues demonstrate a predominantly linear response and produce return echoes at the same frequency as the transmitted pulse. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 2. Diagram illustrates microbubble response to ultrasound pulses of various pressures (power). (A) At low pressure (very low mechanical index [MI]), microbubbles contract and rarefact proportional to the pressure wave (linear oscillation). (B) At slightly higher pressures (but still low MI), microbubble oscillation is nonlinear, meaning rarefaction is out of proportion to compression. This produces strong harmonics of the fundamental frequency. (C) At even higher pulse pressures (high MI), microbubbles become unstable and fall apart, releasing the gas core. This phenomenon may be unintentional or purposeful, resulting in loss of the microbubble-related signal. Download as PowerPoint Another unique physical property of microbubbles is their fragility. At peak pressures greater than 500 kPa, the degree of microbubble oscillation disrupts the shell, with immediate release of the gas core and loss of signal. Therefore, a US machine’s contrast mode relies on low-power imaging, as measured by using the mechanical index (MI), a manufacturer-specific estimate of absorbed acoustic pressure. At a low MI (<0.1), microbubbles demonstrate optimal oscillation to generate a signal while minimizing microbubble destruction. However, a higher MI (>0.5) leads to accelerated microbubble destruction. High-MI techniques may be used to purposefully clear microbubbles from the field of view; this is known as a burst, or flash, whereby a short rapid succession of high-power pulses is transmitted (Fig 3; Movies 1, 2). This technique facilitates a brief high-signal image acquisition, during which microbubbles are destroyed, with subsequent loss of the microbubble-related signal. In addition, this technique can be leveraged to evaluate enhancement patterns and the refill rate of lesions; this is especially useful for characterizing rapidly enhancing liver lesions. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 3. Purposeful microbubble destruction in a 61-year-old man with a history of urothelial cancer and transaminitis, in whom multiple hepatic lesions (found to be cysts) were evaluated on dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the right liver lobe. (A) Images obtained 2 minutes 39 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride lipid-type A microspheres (Lumason, Bracco Diagnostics) show fairly homogeneous enhancement of the hepatic parenchyma. (B) The US system’s burst, or flash, function is activated, resulting in a marked increase in signal throughout the contrast-only image. (C) Immediately afterward, the majority of the signal throughout the liver has been lost (destruction of microbubbles), with early filling of some hepatic vessels (arrows). (D) Turning in an orthogonal plane reveals a dark vertical band (arrowheads) in the original imaging plane, indicating the location of destroyed microbubbles. Download as PowerPoint Movie 1: Flash-reperfusion technique. For lesions or structures with rapid enhancement, such as flash-filling hemangioma and focal nodular hyperplasia, a high-power sequence of ultrasound pulses (ie, burst or flash) can be used to purposefully destroy the microbubbles—and thus the contrast agent–related signal—within the field of view, allowing new microbubbles to enter the structure so that the pattern of enhancement can be re-assessed. In this case, an isoechoic liver lesion has a spoke-wheel pattern of arterial phase hyperenhancement with centrifugal fill-in, characteristic of focal nodular hyperplasia. Download Original Video (48.11 MB) Movie 2: In-plane microbubble destruction can occur after prolonged imaging in a single plane, or after using a high-power sequence of ultrasound pulses (burst or flash), which destroys microbubbles—and thus the contrast agent–related signal—within the field of view. After moving the transducer into the orthogonal plane, a narrow band of relatively low signal signifies the area where microbubbles were destroyed, a form of pseudowashout. Download Original Video (41.77 MB) CEUS Image Generation CEUS image generation relies on the differences in response between microbubbles (nonlinear) and the surrounding tissues (predominantly linear). US devices with contrast agent–specific imaging software take advantage of these differences. Most US unit manufacturers use a combination of phase and amplitude modulation and harmonic filtering to null the linear responses from background tissues and produce contrast-only images (Fig 4). This technique, also known as real-time tissue subtraction, provides exquisite microbubble-specific signal relative to background, allowing the administration of very small contrast agent doses (less than a few milliliters for most agents) and the detection of even a single microbubble in vivo (Movie 3) (13). OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 4. Diagram illustrates the phase-inversion technique, one method used in most CEUS modes. In normal scanning, a single transmitted pulse produces backscatter signal from tissues and microbubbles. Most structures, such as the majority of background tissues, respond linearly, meaning that the backscatter signal is centered at fundamental frequency (f), while structures that respond nonlinearly, such as microbubbles, produce strong harmonics (2f, 3f, etc). With phase inversion, two pulses of opposite phase are transmitted nearly simultaneously. The backscatter signal from linearly responding structures is nulled, while the backscatter signal from non–linearly responding structures is relatively amplified. This is one mechanism for achieving background tissue subtraction and creating contrast-only images. Download as PowerPoint Movie 3: Detection of single circulating microbubbles is accomplished by leveraging the nonlinear acoustic response of microbubbles compared with the primarily linear response of the surrounding tissues. By suppressing signal from the background tissues, exquisite signal-to-background is achieved, allowing even a 5-μm microbubble to be detected. Download Original Video (10.15 MB) The contrast-only image is often presented in an orange or sepia hue and can be displayed on its own, in dual-screen mode side by side with a gray-scale image (Figs 5, E1), or as an overlay on the gray-scale image. In contrast mode, the reference gray-scale image is reconstructed from the contrast pulse sequence and is generally of lower quality compared with a standard B-mode gray-scale image. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 5. Dual display mode CEUS in a 49-year-old man with hepatitis C virus and HIV infection, who was found to have sepsis and a liver lesion suspicious for abscess. Dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images through the right liver lobe were obtained 57 seconds after intravenous administration of 1.5 mL of sulfur hexafluoride microspheres. On the contrast-only image, the lesion (arrows) is nearly completely nonenhancing, indicating nonvascularized material (*), as seen in cysts and other fluid collections such as hematomas and abscesses. Fluid aspiration revealed frankly purulent material. Download as PowerPoint

Technical Parameters and Image Optimization Contrast-enhanced Imaging Mode Settings Achieving an optimal balance between technical imaging parameters, scanning technique, and UCA dose is key to obtaining high-quality CEUS images. To perform high-quality imaging and minimize artifacts, it is important to understand and optimize the technical parameters that influence the contrast mode images (Table 1). Although the specific names and attributes of the adjustable parameters may vary among manufacturers, contemporary contrast settings are generally preoptimized and require minimal, if any, adjustment. User-adjustable settings may include output power, image gain, frame rate, focal zone, transducer frequency, depth, and sector width. OPEN IN VIEWER Table 1: Scanning Parameters and Techniques Affecting CEUS Study Quality Output Power.—The amplitude of the transmitted pulse, referred to as output power and related to the MI, must be low enough during contrast-enhanced imaging to minimize bubble destruction while maintaining an adequate signal-to-noise ratio (14). US machine vendors provide standard output power settings that are optimized for most scenarios, which generally do not require adjusting. In select situations (deep lesions, obese patients, hepatic steatosis), it may be appropriate to increase the output power to achieve additional penetration (2,14,15). Although increasing the output power will result in increased backscatter signal from microbubbles, it may also increase image noise and accentuate some artifacts such as pseudoenhancement. (See the “Bubble Destruction” section.) Increasing output power also accelerates microbubble destruction, decreases the contrast agent half-life, and may cause pseudowashout (2,15). If the output power is too low, the contrast mode image will be too dark. Image Gain.—Image gain, a postprocessing parameter, reflects the overall amplification of returning echoes and affects image brightness. When the contrast-enhanced image gain is correctly adjusted, the organ or tissue of interest is black or nearly black, leaving only a few highly echogenic interfaces visible. When the image gain is set too high, the precontrast image shows diffuse bright pixels, indicating noise (Fig 6) (14). When the gain is set too low, the contrast-enhanced image may be too dark for interpretation. Therefore, it is recommended that highly echogenic interfaces, such as fascial planes, organ capsules, vessel walls, bowel gas, and calcifications, remain visible (Fig 6). As it is difficult to eliminate all echoes from the contrast-only image before contrast agent administration, careful comparison of the precontrast versus postcontrast images may be necessary to ensure that a signal seen on the postinjection postcontrast image is from microbubbles and not due to artifact. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 6. Importance of precontrast gain settings demonstrated in a 57-year-old man with a left renal mass and widespread metastatic disease who presented for biopsy. (A–C) Sequential dual-screen contrast-only (left) and gray-scale (right) contrast mode US images were obtained through the left renal mass before contrast agent administration. With a correctly optimized image gain (A), echogenic interfaces such as fascial planes (arrows) and calcifications (arrowheads) should remain bright. With the gain set too low (B), noise is amplified, producing a diffuse haze over the entire image. With the gain set too high (C), noise is amplified, producing a diffuse haze over the entire image. The biopsy needle trajectory was changed and viable tissue was obtained. Download as PowerPoint TEACHING POINT The image gain should be adjusted before UCA administration. Adjusting the gain after UCA administration is generally not recommended. Although increasing the gain can result in increased brightness from the microbubbles, it also amplifies noise, and differentiating enhancing from nonenhancing structures may be difficult (2,15). If quantitative perfusion measurements are needed, the contrast presets should not be adjusted, as this may alter the input signal and lead to errors in time-intensity curve analysis (16). Similar to overall gain adjustments, time-gain-compensation adjustments should be made before the contrast agent administration to achieve image uniformity from the near field to the far field (Fig 7). Fine adjustments can be made to compensate for excessive attenuation by amplifying the signal from the far field, although these adjustments should be made with caution, given the risk of amplifying noise and pseudoenhancement. Download as PowerPoint Frame Rate.—The frame rate is the number of images displayed per unit of time, expressed in hertz. The frame rate generally is automatically set to the maximal value that is allowable, depending on the sector width, image depth, and other settings. Some systems allow a user to decrease the frame rate (sometimes referred to as the pulse repetition frequency). Although high frame rates allow higher temporal resolution, they may also lead to accelerated microbubble destruction (2). In contrast mode, frame rates may be reduced owing to the multipulse techniques used for contrast-enhanced imaging. Reducing the frame rate further may minimize microbubble destruction, and this may be helpful in evaluating lesions with slow washout, such as some hepatocellular carcinomas. Conversely, rapidly enhancing lesions may need a higher frame rate for adequate assessment of high-flow structures or lesions (17). Focal Zone.—Ultrasound energy deposition and, thus, microbubble disruption, is not uniform throughout the image. Much of the ultrasound beam energy is concentrated at the focal zone. To minimize microbubble destruction, the focal zone should generally be positioned well beyond (deep to) the target. Many users place the focal zone at the lowest depth in the field of view. When assessing focal liver lesions, it may be appropriate to place the focal zone just below the lesion of interest to balance spatial resolution and microbubble destruction (17). Transducer Frequency.—Optimal microbubble resonance is achieved at lower frequencies (1–3 MHz). Although imaging at lower frequencies improves penetration, it negatively impacts spatial resolution. Higher-frequency curvilinear transducers and high-frequency linear transducers may be used for CEUS to improve spatial resolution; however, the signal from microbubbles may not be as strong, resulting in an overall darker contrast-enhanced image (Fig 8). Also, at higher frequencies, a smaller distribution of microbubble sizes will be stimulated. For these reasons, a higher contrast agent dose—up to 1.5 to two times the dose of that for the liver or kidney—may be needed. OPEN IN VIEWER Figure 8.
